Instruction: Write a tweet highlighting impressive founders and their achievements, mentioning their 'Core group' affiliation and encouraging others to form similar groups, in an informative and enthusiastic tone.

Previous posts for style reference:
- "Alright so Alfred Nobel.

He invented dynamite.

When his brother died, a newspaper mistakenly ran an obituary for Alfred. 

The obituary said Albert was the Merchant of Death because of his invention.

That made him feel horrible.

So, he put 95% of his net worth, which was like $200m in today's money, into a trust.

And then he created an annual prize called the Nobel Peace prize.

Then he told the trustees "give away about $1m/year to people who impact humanity the most."

And thus the Nobel Peace. 

The reason it interests me: instead of giving money to charity or leaving money to family. The idea of creating an award to incentivize action and make it almost like a game...that's pretty interesting to me.

On the pod I break down a few of these awards and if they've been effective. 

What do you think?"
- "Oasis just announced a 2025 UK tour.

I will be in attendance!"

This is a Hampton Core group in Austin.

Jason Cohen - founded WP Engine, grew it to hundreds of millions in revenue. 

Brad - co-founded a bootstrapped co to close to half a million in revenue selling IT to the government

Rakesh - CEO of two companies: SnapStream (used by The Daily Show & the US Senate) and Piping Technology (9 figures in revenue)

Michail - founded Backd, a fintech doing tens of millions in revenue. Also runs a bootstrapped SaaS company

Casey - co-founded Brand Honey, a DTC health holding company. Previously helped scale a brand to $50M/yr

Adam - co-founded Boom & Bucket, a marketplace disrupting how $300B in heavy equipment gets bought and sold

They're members of Hampton.

They meet monthly to discuss business + life.

This photo is from their Core group dinner in Austin (shared w/ permission).

Get you a Core group. Makes building stuff much, much better + less lonely (regardless if it's w/ Hampton or not).

Instruction: Write a personal anecdote about a friend who is the opposite of the author, highlighting the friend's humility and conversational skills, in a reflective and admiring tone.

I have a dear, dear friend. His name's @_jacksmith. 

If you've ever met Jack, he's often time the quietest person in the room. 

He'll rarely talks about himself (unless asked) and is a wonderful conversationalist because he asks tons of great questions. 

Jack sold his company for ~$850m when he was like 29.

At least a dozen times, I've intro-d Jack to buddies at parties. Afterward, they'll tell me how much they like him and will ask his story. 

I'll tell 'em yeah he's a super successful person this and that. 

They're always shocked. There's even been times when they'll talk to him about business or even the industry he sold his company in. 

He won't talk about his past and just keep asking them questions, making them feel good. 

I'm the opposite of Jack. I'll talk your ear off and will never fail to mention stuff I hope impresses you!

But often I have to remind myself: be more like Jack.

Instruction: Write a reflective and cautionary tweet about startup CEO salaries, sharing personal experience and linking to a survey.

How much should a startup CEO pay themselves?

When my last co did $2m, I paid myself ~$24,000/year. At around $5m, I paid myself like $50,000. 

That was VERY stupid of me. Made me feel defensive, like money was scarce, and impatient for an exit. 

Very dumb. Very, very dumb. 

Had I known what I know now, I 100% would have paid myself much more. I legit felt poor and it made me make stupid decisions, even when the biz had +$1m in the bank account. 

We surveyed a bunch of members and asked them what they pay themselves. Ranges from companies with $0 revenue (but some funding) all the way up to $100m/year in rev.

https://t.co/t6lNvpy6UX

Instruction: Write a tweet summarizing a podcast interview with Bryan Johnson about his personal finances and capital allocation, focusing on key figures and his investment philosophy, in an informative and direct style.

A while back I interviewed Bryan Johnson. He did a full breakdown of his personal finances + how he thinks about money.

Some of the numbers:
- Braintree exit (personal take): ~$300M after tax
- Built Braintree for 7 years (bootstrapped 4–5 years)
- Paid himself almost nothing while building
- net worth: ~$200–300M
- Post-exit personal spend (ages 34–44): ~$10–20K / month

Capital Allocation
- Deep-tech venture fund: $100M
- Kernel (brain interface): ~$60–64M
- Blueprint: $20–30M
- Total deployed into private projects: ~$160M
- Net worth illiquid: ~60–70%
- Biggest regret: Not staying more liquid

We also did a full breakdown of how he thinks about legacy and wealth creation, which was the most interesting part.

If you want the full breakdown, check it: https://t.co/KG9S5nZKdv

- "Oasis just announced a 2025 UK tour.

I will be in attendance!"

I asked this question in Hampton's Slack: 

who here scaled to $20-$50m+ working 40 hours a week?

Replies (anonymized):

Founder A who crossed $50M:

"My secret was having 4 kids with intense schedules and a great management team. The family commitment is a great forcing function."

His 40 hours now are less chaotic than his old 60 ever were.

Founder B - crossed $20M, pacing $30M:

"95% of decisions don't matter, and virtually all can be reversed. Most founders who grind crazy hours associate identity and prestige with being involved in every decision.

Letting go of that need for control gave me back most of my time."

Founder C built two companies to $15-20M:

First one: worked like a dog. Most of the extra hours were learning and putting out fires he created.

Second one: under 40 hours once he had a focused strategy and competent leaders.

"It's your competency at the thing you're doing and the number of things you're doing."

Founder D - just crossed $20M:

Got 12 employees onto the cap table as small owners through an internal succession plan.

"Those employee-owners solve problems before they ever reach me. I'm down to 40 hours, but those hours are also much less chaotic than my previous 60."

Founder E - $20M+ agency:

Instead of chasing volume with small clients, they landed a few massive enterprise accounts. Same work unlocks 8 figures of spend.

Then added an exec layer to shift from client focus to business focus. Hours dropped every year.

Founder F - $20M+:

"Ruthless simplification. Every quarter we cut things."
Scariest move: firing their largest client. The client was consuming the entire team and was misaligned with what they were good at.

Revenue went up after.

Founder G:

"Hire exceptional people. Not every hire needs to be exceptional, but a few will make problems that would normally fall on you disappear.

Why do others fail? They tell themselves they've hired exceptional people but have never actually worked with one. Their standards are unknowingly low."

----

Anyone else done it? Wanna add anything?

- "My fellow fatties:

2 macadamia nuts stuffed into a fresh date = awesome M&M alternative."

I've got a buddy who sold his company for ~$70m.

So, it was a massive financial success. 

However, he HATED the life he made for himself while running it.

His company felt like a prison:

- 16-hour days. Skipped his honeymoon. 
- Micromanaged everything. 
- Built his entire identity around the selling.

After selling, like all of us idiots, he started his second company.

My buddy, btw, is @jnpayne.

But for his next company, he made a bunch of rules. Rules so that he'd be happier during the build process.

- The hours were 8 to 5. 
- No weekends. 
- Fully remote. 
- No meetings where he wasn't essential.
- And a month-long family trip to Europe every summer with his wife and three kids.

Recently, he did something super cool: moved his entire family of five to Spain.

Other things Josh did that I like:

- he removed himself from operational meetings
- scaled every 1:1 to monthly
- muted Slack channels where he wasn't essential
- assigned owners to projects and stopped poking around. 

His words: "I frankly just dropped the ball on a lot of things."

But...it seems to be working. 

His new company, its called Onward, and they're very profitable with 20 people.

I LOVE hearing stories like this.

Because the entire point of this business thing, at least for me, is to put your dent into the world but doing it your way.

On twitter, podcasts, etc...there are so many rules that we take for granted. So we often just fit into a box of business building. 

But I've done so many interviews with baller founders + see it in Hampton...there are 10,000's ways to kill it.

“I am fully sensible of the many difficulties under which I labor, and of my own deficiencies. I cannot promise myself success.”

Yet, he was exactly what we needed. The war likely could not have been won without him. 

He spent nearly 7 years away from home, fighting. Normally, generals always spent summers away.

Then when it came time to elect a our first president, he didn't even want to run.

Someone nominated him, he didn't run. Then he received every single electoral vote.

But after two terms, he retired, when there were no term limits. This shocked people because they wanted him to stay in power. 

He said: The power under the Constitution will always be in the people. It is entrusted for certain defined purposes, and for a certain limited period, to representatives of their own choosing.

That's a damn servant leader. A stark contrast to what we have now.

I think the American Revolution is the most important event since the birth of Christ in all of world history.

It turned the world upside down.

Before this moment, everyone was a subject under the rule of somebody else.

This idea has had powerful effects.

It set in motion revolutions for 200+ years—all trying to give new expression to the belief that all men are created equal… endowed with unalienable rights.

It was the beginning of something absolutely new in the world.

The colonies were divided:

New Hampshire vs Georgia (practically different countries)

Yet out of division came unity.

They still found a way to come together.

Instruction: Write a tweet announcing an increase in membership revenue requirements for Hampton, adopting a transparent and slightly self-aware tone.

We’re making a change to Hampton’s membership requirements.

Raising revenue minimum form $1m-$3m.

When we started, it was for marketers. We had a few join, but quickly realized that not many of them wanted it.

When we decided to make it for founders, we knew there had to be some things to make it legit. So, we set some minimums for the businesses we consider.

I think it’s been wonderful and we have a lot of amazing members.

But we felt it’s time for the bar to go up a little bit more.

In December of 2022, we changed our requirements from $1-2m. But we got scared and quickly went back after a couple of weeks. That might have been a mistake, I dunno.

But starting today, it’s going from $1m to $3m for rev and from $5m to $10m for past exit size for new applicants.

If you’re already talking to us about membership, you’re good with the old requirements.

Why are we changing it?

Because we’re starting to see $5-$20m businesses become more of the base.

I’m not saying businesses outside of that aren’t great. They are!

When I was running the Hustle there would have been many years where I wouldn’t have been able to join something like this. But it would have pissed me off to go further. So, there you go if you’re mad about the change.

We think this community has been special and will continue to be. @jspeiser  and I started this to be around incredible people and we’re lucky we are.

Do I have any punk rock followers?

If that's you, you're gonna think this is the coolest story. 

So I went to college at Belmont University, a music school in Nashville.

One day in an accounting class I'm sitting next to this punk rock looking kid named Jordan. Long hair, tight jeans...he looked cool. 

We have this conservative, Jamaican professor. 

She goes around asking people's story on the 1st day of class. 

She gets to Jordan and asks what he's into.

"I'm in a punk rock band. Its called Diarrhea Planet."

I start giggling because he says it with a straight face, no shame and she's a pretty uptight woman. 

She asks if they have any songs she'd be into.

He goes, "yeah our most popular song is Ghost with a Boner, you'd dig it."

It was so funny because he was serious and not shy about it. 

So during class I go to their website. They didn't own their URL, were using like a free wordpress URL.

So, I buy diarrhea planet .com right there. 

A few days later I'm like hey Jordan, I bought your URL. I can give you it for free, just let me know your email.

He kinda brushed it off. and we talked about something else. 

Well a few years later they start getting popular. In the punk world, they have a huge cult following.

I'm living in SF at the time and they're coming to tour there. 

I hadn't talk to em for years and I email 'em hey its the guy who owns your domain name, do you guys want it? I'll give it to you for free. Intro me to who handles your website and its easy. Also, if you need a place to sleep, I got you!

They don't know much about tech. But he's like "hey, yes we'd love to stay at your place to save some cash."

So that night after the show I set up their website so they can use the domain name. I paid for it for like 10+ years or something then gave them ownership.

And to say thanks, they gave me free tickets whenever I wanted to go to a show.

I used to bring dates to their shows and they'd call me out on stage and make me look cool. It was so badass. I never looked cooler.

So, if you're a punk rock fan, I just wanted to take a second to brag...I'M NOT JUST AN INTERNET NERD, I'M COOL.

Also...LONG LIVE DIARRHEA PLANET!

- "I have a dear, dear friend. His name's @_jacksmith. 

If you've ever met Jack, he's often time the quietest person in the room. 

He'll rarely talks about himself (unless asked) and is a wonderful conversationalist because he asks tons of great questions. 

Jack sold his company for ~$850m when he was like 29.

At least a dozen times, I've intro-d Jack to buddies at parties. Afterward, they'll tell me how much they like him and will ask his story. 

I'll tell 'em yeah he's a super successful person this and that. 

They're always shocked. There's even been times when they'll talk to him about business or even the industry he sold his company in. 

He won't talk about his past and just keep asking them questions, making them feel good. 

I'm the opposite of Jack. I'll talk your ear off and will never fail to mention stuff I hope impresses you!

But often I have to remind myself: be more like Jack."

As I progress in my career and rack up some wins (and losses!), my opinion on luck has changed. 

Lucks been a huge factor in many of my wins. 

I didn’t used to think luck was a factor. It is!
